content.body: An inequality states that one expression is greater than or less than another. Unlike equations, inequalities have a range of solutions.

Rules: Add/subtract same value to both sides: inequality sign stays same
Multiply/divide by POSITIVE number: inequality sign stays same
Multiply/divide by NEGATIVE number: inequality sign REVERSES
Example: −2x > 6 → x < −3 (sign reversed when dividing by −2)
NUMBER LINE: Open circle (○) for strict inequality (< or >). Closed circle (●) for ≤ or ≥.

REGIONS ON A GRAPH:
• Dotted line for strict inequality (< or >)
• Solid line for ≤ or ≥
• Shade the region that satisfies the inequality
• Test a point (e.g. origin) to check which side to shade

content.title: Solving an Inequality
content.body:
Solve: 3x − 7 > 8
3x > 15
x > 5
Number line: open circle at 5, arrow to the right

questions.question: Solve: 2x + 3 < 11
When you multiply both sides of an inequality by a negative number:
questions.explanation: Multiplying or dividing by negative reverses the inequality direction. E.g. −x < 3 → x > −3

questions.question: Solve: −3x ≥ 12
questions.explanation: Divide by −3 (negative): ≥ reverses to ≤. x ≤ −4

questions.question: Which represents x > 3 on a number line?
questions.explanation: x > 3 (strict): open circle at 3. Greater than = right arrow.

questions.question: Solve: 5 − 2x < 1
questions.explanation: −2x < −4. Divide by −2 (reverse): x > 2

questions.question: The region y > 2x is found:
questions.explanation: y > 2x means y values are more than the line — above the line.

questions.question: For the inequality x + y ≤ 5, the line is drawn:
questions.explanation: ≤ (less than OR equal to) means points on the line are included — draw solid line.

questions.question: Solve: 4x − 5 ≥ 3x + 2
The solution to −1 < x ≤ 4 includes:
questions.explanation: −1 < x ≤ 4: x is strictly greater than −1 and up to and including 4. So x = 4 is included.

questions.question: To find which region to shade for y < x + 1, test origin (0,0):
questions.explanation: Substitute (0,0): 0 < 0+1 = 1 ✓. Origin satisfies inequality → shade the side containing origin.

questions.question: Solve the double inequality: 2 ≤ 3x − 1 < 8
questions.explanation: Add 1: 3 ≤ 3x < 9. Divide by 3: 1 ≤ x < 3

questions.question: Which integers satisfy 2 < x ≤ 6?
questions.explanation: x is strictly greater than 2 (so not 2) and up to and including 6. Integers: 3, 4, 5, 6.

questions.question: The region satisfying x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0, and x + y ≤ 4 is:
questions.explanation: Three constraints form a triangular feasible region in the first quadrant with vertices (0,0), (4,0), (0,4).

questions.question: Solve: (x − 2)/3 < 4
questions.explanation: Multiply both sides by 3: x − 2 < 12. x < 14

questions.question: What does the closed circle (●) mean on a number line?
questions.explanation: Closed/filled circle means the endpoint value IS part of the solution (≤ or ≥ inequality).

questions.question: The region ABOVE the line y = 3 satisfies:
questions.explanation: Above the line y = 3 means y values are more than 3, i.e., y > 3.

questions.question: The region satisfying x > 2 AND y < 3 is:
options: Right of x=2 and below y=3
Right of x=2 and above y=3
Left of x=2 and below y=3
questions.answer: Right of x=2 and below y=3
questions.explanation: x > 2 = right of vertical line x=2. y < 3 = below horizontal line y=3.

questions.question: The difference between x > 5 and x ≥ 5 is:
questions.explanation: Strict inequality x > 5 means x cannot equal 5. Non-strict x ≥ 5 includes x = 5.

questions.question: Inequalities and the feasible region from linear programming both use:
options: Shaded regions to show solution sets
questions.answer: Shaded regions to show solution sets
questions.explanation: Both topics use graphical regions — inequalities identify solution sets; linear programming finds optimal values within those regions.
